data.description:
Problem statement
-----------------
We have an existing install/start script for the proxy, but operators and developers need a tiny CLI to manage the running instance (start, stop, restart) reliably across user-local installs. The CLI should provide simple commands to control the proxy process and basic status/log access.

Users
-----
- Operators and local developers who install the proxy on a host and need a consistent, low-friction way to start, stop, and restart the running proxy.
- Support engineers diagnosing local installs.

Example user stories
- As an operator, I can run `proxyctl start` to start the proxy using the repository `start-llama.sh` / installed start script so the proxy process is running and reachable.
- As an operator, I can run `proxyctl stop` to stop the running proxy process cleanly.
- As an operator, I can run `proxyctl restart` to restart the proxy, preserving any necessary state.
- As an operator, I can run `proxyctl status` to see whether the proxy is running and view its PID and basic health info.
- As an operator, I can run `proxyctl logs` to tail the proxy logs for troubleshooting.

Success criteria
----------------
- A small CLI `proxyctl` (bash) is added to the repo and documented; it supports `start`, `stop`, `restart`, `status`, and `logs` commands.
- `start` uses the configured start script (defaults to repo root `start-llama.sh`) and records a PIDfile or other indicator so `stop`/`status` operate reliably.
- `stop` cleanly terminates the running proxy process (SIGINT/SIGTERM) and returns a clear exit code and message.
- `status` returns exit code 0 and human-friendly output when the proxy is running (shows PID and start time) and non-zero plus message when not running.
- Basic docs updated: `proxy/README.md` or `HOST_INSTALL.md` contains usage examples and install location (e.g., `/usr/local/bin/proxyctl`).

Constraints
-----------
- Implementation language: Bash shell script (aligned with existing scripts) — chosen for minimal dependencies and easy install in `/usr/local/bin`.
- Target environment: user-local process management (no systemd by default). If operators want systemd, create a follow-up work item to add systemd unit management.
- Must respect repository configuration: use `proxy/config.yaml` `llama_start_script` when available, otherwise default to repo `start-llama.sh`.
- Must avoid requiring elevated privileges (no sudo) for the default flow.

Existing state
--------------
- The repo already contains startup scripts and docs referencing `start-llama.sh` and host install notes: `proxy/README.md`, `proxy/CONTAINERS.md`, `proxy/HOST_INSTALL.md`, and the top-level `start-llama.sh` script.
- Diagnostic and service-related helper scripts already exist under `proxy/scripts/` including `diagnose-llama-proxy-startup.sh` and removal scripts for systemd units.
- Several work items reference proxy startup, systemd unit removal, and router-mode integration (see Related work below).

Desired change
--------------
- Add a minimal `proxyctl` bash CLI to the repository that provides `start|stop|restart|status|logs` commands and uses the repo's start script by default. Include installation instructions and example usage in `proxy/README.md`.

Related work (manual list)
-------------------------
- proxy/scripts/remove-user-llama-proxy-service.sh — script to remove per-user systemd service files (useful reference for stopping services).
- proxy/scripts/diagnose-llama-proxy-startup.sh — diagnostic helper that enumerates processes and may provide useful status detection heuristics.
- proxy/README.md — documents `llama_start_script` config and current start guidance; update this with `proxyctl` usage.
- proxy/config.yaml — contains `llama_start_script` default path; `proxyctl` should respect this config.
- proxy/CONTAINERS.md, proxy/container/Containerfile — examples of containerized start flows; note but not in scope for default CLI.
- HOST_INSTALL.md — host install guidance referencing `start-llama.sh`.
- Work items potentially related (from Worklog search):
  - Remove systemd service files for llama-proxy (LP-0MN6JRADW0NL3E5W) — completed; check for unit names.
  - Remove systemd/service management from proxy startup script (LP-0MN6JEIFR034RI4C) — completed; relevant historical context.
  - Startup scripts & container notes (LP-0MN8LU5B9006ZIAY) — blocked; contains guidance on flags and start script usage.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
---------------------------------------
- Q: "Which environments should the CLI manage?" — Answer (user): "User-local process (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply via intake interview. Final: yes.
- Q: "What language should the CLI be implemented in?" — Answer (user): "Bash shell script (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply via intake interview. Final: yes.
- Q: "Which additional commands should the CLI provide beyond start/stop/restart?" — Answer (user): "status + logs (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply via intake interview. Final: yes.
data.title: Proxy CLI: manage running proxy (start/stop/restart)

data.description:
Problem statement
-----------------
Callers should be able to use short, human-friendly model aliases in API requests (for example `"embeddings"`, `"plan"`, `"code"`) that map to configured model presets, so clients do not need to know internal preset ids or implementation details.

Users
-----
- Frontend engineers and web UI tests: want to call `/v1/embeddings` or pass `model: "embeddings"` or `model: "plan"` from the UI without knowing internal preset ids.
- Backend services and scripts: want stable, low-latency access to embedding and task-specific models using simple names.
- Operators / infra teams: want these aliases to be configurable in `models.ini` or `proxy/config.yaml` so mappings can be changed without code changes.

Success criteria
----------------
- The proxy accepts `model` values of `embeddings`, `plan`, and `code` (case-insensitive) and routes requests to the configured preset.
- Aliases are configurable via `models.ini` / `proxy/config.yaml` (no hardcoding required) and documented in `proxy/README.md`.
- Existing alias behaviour (wildcards, exact-vs-wildcard precedence) is preserved and extended to new aliases; unit and integration tests exercise the new aliases.
- `/v1/models` and the web UI list aliases or otherwise make it discoverable which friendly names are supported.

Constraints
-----------
- Must be configurable using existing config patterns (`models.ini`, `proxy/config.yaml`) and follow existing alias semantics (fnmatch wildcards, exact match precedence).
- Must not change existing routing logic or break existing alias mappings (backwards compatible).
- Router-mode and embeddings-specific preloads must continue to work (do not regress router preloading behavior).

Existing state
--------------
- The proxy already supports aliases and wildcard patterns in `proxy/config.yaml` / `models.ini` and documents them in `proxy/README.md` (see `models: ... aliases:` and wildcard rules).
- The codebase contains special handling for the `embeddings` alias and router-mode preloading (see `proxy/server.py` and `proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py`).
- There are existing work items and spikes related to embeddings co-residency, router-mode, and model lifecycle (see Related work below).

Desired change
--------------
- Ensure friendly aliases `embeddings`, `plan`, and `code` can be declared and mapped in config files and that the proxy will translate those aliases to the configured preset when routing requests.
- Add or update unit and integration tests for the new aliases (including embeddings endpoint tests and `/v1/models` listing).
- Update documentation (`proxy/README.md`, `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md`) and the Web UI to surface supported friendly names.

Related work
------------
Potentially related docs:
- `proxy/README.md` — documents alias support, wildcard rules, router-mode, and the embeddings endpoint.
- `proxy/config.yaml` — example configuration showing `llama_router_preload` and an `embeddings` entry.
- `models.ini` — contains `embeddings = true` and is referenced by the proxy config.
- `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md` — guidance for adding models including `aliases` and an integration test example.

Potentially related work items:
- Routing & alias behaviour for embeddings (LP-0MN8LU54M00774GG) — routing behaviour for embeddings and aliases.
- Add an embeddings specific model (LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC) — completed; example and precedent for embedding models.
- Router-mode llama-server integration (LP-0MNA7FYH4001ZUR4) — implements router API and preloading semantics used by embeddings.
- Model lifecycle: embed + primary slot (LP-0MNA7G07U0020NDC) — lifecycle changes to support co-loaded embedding slot.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
--------------------------------------
- Q: "Which friendly model aliases should this work item support initially? (select multiple if needed)" — Answer (user): "Embeddings (recommended), Plan (recommended), Code (recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Should aliases be configurable via existing config (models.ini / proxy/config.yaml) or implemented as a static mapping in code?" — Answer (user): "Configurable via models.ini / proxy/config.yaml (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

Research notes / evidence (agent inference):
- `proxy/README.md` documents alias support and wildcard rules (see `models: ... aliases:` section).
- `proxy/config.yaml` contains `llama_router_preload` with `"embeddings"` and an `embeddings_model` setting; `models.ini` has `embeddings = true`.
- `proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py` contains tests that POST with `model: "embeddings"` to `/v1/embeddings`.

2. Check proxy logs, app logs, network errors and metrics.

Expected behaviour: Proxy forwards requests to upstream and returns timely responses to requestor.

- Provide reproduction steps or a failing test/script if applicable.
- Propose a minimal remediation and list any follow-ups.

Suggested approach:

- Search codebase for proxy-related code and recent changes.
- Collect relevant logs (proxy, backend services) for the timestamps of failed requests.
- Check for timeouts, upstream errors (502/504), connection resets, or resource exhaustion.

data.description:
Make model-loading non-blocking in request handlers so client connections are not held while models start.

Changes:
- Schedule model loads in background when a request would trigger a model switch or load.
- Immediately return HTTP 503 with Retry-After to the caller when a background load is started.
- Log background load start/completion and expose a simple in-memory marker for loads-in-progress.

Acceptance criteria:
- Requests that would have triggered a model load return 503 immediately and do not hang.
- Background model load proceeds and, on success, subsequent requests are served.
- Relevant log lines are emitted when background load starts and completes.

Files to change: proxy/server.py (request handlers + background loader helpers)

data.description:
Problem statement
-----------------
Provide a small HTTP GET endpoint that returns a JSON object describing the current status of the local llama.cpp (llama-server) process. By default the endpoint will indicate whether there is an active query and whether a model switch/load is in progress; it should also return the currently loaded model name and whether the server process is running.

Users
-----
- Operators and local developers who need a machine-readable status check for monitoring, health pages, or local tooling.
- Frontend pages and automated tests that render model status or model-related metrics (example user story: "As a frontend engineer I can poll /llama/local/status and show whether llama-server is running and which model is loaded").

Success criteria
----------------
- A GET endpoint is available at `/llama/local/status` and responds 200 with a JSON body for a healthy request.
- Default JSON response includes the fields: `active_query` (bool), `model_switch_in_progress` (bool), `current_model` (string|null), and `llama_server_running` (bool).
- Endpoint behaviour is deterministic: when llama-server is stopped `llama_server_running` is false and other fields are null/false as appropriate; during a model switch `model_switch_in_progress` is true.
- A unit/integration test exists that calls the endpoint and asserts the presence and types of the required fields.
- The change is traceable in the worklog and references the updated test(s) or UI tests if modified.

Constraints
-----------
- Endpoint will be mounted on the existing proxy FastAPI app and may be considered internal — no additional auth is required for the initial implementation.
- Keep the response small and stable (avoid including large or changing payloads by default). Additional optional fields (n_ctx, kv_cache_tokens, token counters) may be added later as separate work.
- Avoid introducing breaking changes to existing `/health` and SSE endpoints; prefer to reuse existing helpers (e.g., `query_llama_status()` and SSE broadcasting) where possible.

Existing state
--------------
- The proxy already exposes a `/health` endpoint (see `proxy/server.py:2948`) that returns `current_model` and `llama_server_running`.
- The proxy streams status updates via SSE on `/events` (see `proxy/server.py:2965`) and a `query_llama_status()` helper exists that queries local llama-server `/model` and `/status` endpoints for additional metadata (`proxy/server.py:332-400+`, `query_llama_status()` defined at ~332).
- Playwright tests already exercise a stats panel and SSE payloads (`proxy/tests/status-stats.spec.js`) and may be updated later to assert or consume the new endpoint if desired.

Desired change
--------------
- Add a new FastAPI route `GET /llama/local/status` that returns JSON with the required default fields.
- Implement the handler by reusing existing helpers: consult `current_model`, `llama_process` state, `model_switch_lock`/background load flags, and `query_llama_status()` as appropriate to populate fields.
- Add a small unit/integration test that requests the endpoint and asserts JSON keys and types.

Related work
------------
Potentially related docs
- `proxy/server.py` — main proxy implementation and helpers (status, health, SSE)  : proxy/server.py
- `proxy/tests/status-stats.spec.js` — Playwright tests that assert SSE payload and stats panel : proxy/tests/status-stats.spec.js
- `proxy/tests/status-bar.spec.js` — existing UI tests that reference the status bar : proxy/tests/status-bar.spec.js
- `.opencode/tmp/intake-draft-Add-stats-to-the-main-page-for-the-currently-loaded-model-LP-0MN5AW8DE1KJVAKX.md` — prior intake for stats-panel work : .opencode/tmp/intake-draft-Add-stats-to-the-main-page-for-the-currently-loaded-model-LP-0MN5AW8DE1KJVAKX.md

Potentially related work items
- Add stats to the main page for the currently loaded model (LP-0MN5AW8DE1KJVAKX) — completed; related UI tests and SSE usage.
- Proxy: router-mode integration (LP-0MNJEG2EH00434ZT) — open; router-mode affects how models/loaded_models are reported.
- Co-residency spike: llama-server API & memory (LP-0MN8SI4S5004AQVX) — in-progress; related to model load semantics and memory measurements.
- Can we load the embed model and another model at the same time? (LP-0MN7T0KFJ004ALGK) — open; relevant to behaviour when multiple models may be present.

Appendix — Clarifying questions and answers
------------------------------------------
- Q: "Which path should the REST endpoint use?" — Answer (user): "/llama/local/status - the \"local\" part can be hard coded for now, it is a placeholder for adding named destinations in a possible future." Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Which fields must be included by default?" — Answer (user): "active_query (bool), model_switch_in_progress (bool), current_model (string), llama_server_running (bool)" Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "What test coverage do you want for this change?" — Answer (user): "Unit/integration test for endpoint (Recommended)" Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
data.title: Provide REST status endpoint for llama.cpp server

Summary:
Ensure the proxy and UI only mark a model as active after the llama-server confirms it is loaded and ready. Keep switching state accurate for admin and background loads.

User story:
As an operator, I want the UI and API to stay in switching state until the requested model is actually loaded so I can trust the active model indicator.

Expected behavior:
- `current_model` only updates to the requested model after readiness is confirmed.
- SSE/status endpoints reflect switching vs ready state correctly.
- UI uses SSE/status signals rather than optimistic updates.
- Background loads still return 503 and schedule loading; admin switches block or provide clear guidance until ready.

Acceptance criteria:
- Admin switch triggers a switching state and UI does not mark active until ready event arrives.
- `current_model` does not change to the requested model during switching and only updates on readiness confirmation.
- Tests cover switching sequence end-to-end (switch -> switching SSE -> ready SSE -> current_model updated).
- No regression to background-load scheduling behavior.

data.description:
Title: Split logging web UI: proxy + llama-server tail

Problem statement
-----------------
The current `/logs` web UI tails only the proxy log. Operators and developers also need live access to the llama-server stdout/stderr log (captured to `llama-server.log`) while troubleshooting model loads and runtime errors. We will split the view into two vertical panes: one showing the existing proxy log tail and one that tails the llama-server log.

Users
-----
- Support engineers and developers debugging model start/load failures and runtime errors. Example story: "As an on-call engineer, I want to see both proxy and llama-server logs side-by-side so I can correlate a failing request in the proxy with the llama-server startup messages."  
- Local operators running the proxy+llama locally using `proxyctl`/scripts who need an in-browser tail for convenience.

Success criteria
----------------
- The `/logs` UI provides two vertically split panes: left pane tails `proxy.log` (existing behavior), right pane tails `llama-server.log` with the same controls (lines, connect/disconnect, clear, download, autoscroll).  
- The llama-server pane streams initial N lines then appends new lines via SSE or a combined server stream; switching panes or disconnecting does not crash the server.  
- Implement server-side support so both sources can be served reliably in development and production (respecting configured `log_dir` and `/var/log/llama-proxy`), including handling rotated/missing files gracefully.  
- UI clearly labels each pane and the downloaded file names (e.g., `proxy.log`, `llama-server.log`).  
- No regression to existing proxy log tailing behavior and automated tests (if present) keep passing.

Constraints
-----------
- The proxy process already implements `/logs/tail` SSE for `proxy.log`; prefer lightweight changes that reuse existing SSE infrastructure.  
- In production the logs may be under `/var/log/llama-proxy` and may require read permissions; do not assume root. The server currently falls back to `./logs`.  
- Avoid large architectural changes (no full websocket rework) unless necessary.  
- WL (worklog) operations could not be completed in this environment due to a local worklog DB migration mismatch; this brief is saved as a draft until WL is available.

Existing state
--------------
- Server: `server.py` implements `/logs` (HTML UI) and `/logs/tail` SSE streaming for `proxy.log`. The UI is a single-pane tail with controls and an injected `window.__INITIAL_STATS` snapshot. (see `server.py` /logs and /logs/tail handlers).  
- Logs: README documents `proxy.log` and `llama-server.log` usage and rotation; `start_llama.sh` and `server.start_llama_server()` manage `llama-server.log` rotation.  
- `proxyctl` has a `logs` command to tail the proxy log locally.  

Desired change
--------------
- Add a second SSE source for `llama-server.log` (options below) and update `/logs` UI to show two vertically split panes with identical controls.  
- Server-side options (implementor choice; recommended server-side merge if feasible):  
  - Option A (recommended by user): Server-side merges proxy + llama logs into a single SSE stream with labeled messages and the UI renders two panes by subscribing to appropriate sources (or the merged stream can include labeled events for both).  
  - Option B: Add a dedicated endpoint `/logs/llama/tail` that mirrors `/logs/tail` but for `llama-server.log`.  
  - Option C: Extend `/logs/tail` with a `?source=` param (e.g. `?source=llama`) and reuse the same handler for both sources.  

Related work (automated report)
-------------------------------
- `server.py` — /logs and /logs/tail handlers: implements existing proxy UI and SSE tail logic; source of most UI and SSE behavior to reuse. (file: `server.py`, lines around `/logs` and `/logs/tail`)  
- `README.md` — documents logs, where `llama-server.log` is kept and rotated; useful for acceptance criteria and commands for operators. (file: `README.md` lines referencing `llama-server.log` and tails)  
- `proxyctl` — CLI `logs` command that tails proxy logs; useful reference for file locations and dev UX. (file: `proxyctl`)  
- `config.yaml` — contains log-related config values (retention, directories). (file: `config.yaml`)  

Implementation notes (suggested)
------------------------------
- Reuse `read_last_n()` helper (in `/logs/tail`) for initial block and the SSE event generator pattern for streaming new lines.  
- Mirror the proxy SSE message shape: initial block as `{'initial': <text>}`, appended messages as `{'line': <text>}`, and counts/tokens events unchanged. For merged streams add a `source` key, e.g., `{'source':'llama','line': '...'} ` or `{'source':'proxy','line':'...'}` when merging server-side.  
- Ensure file-rotation handling is mirrored (detect smaller file size -> reset read position).  
- Downloads should use the original file name when saving (server UI already sets `proxy.log` as download filename).  

Acceptance criteria (developer-concrete)
-------------------------------------
1. Two-pane UI: visiting `/logs` shows two labeled vertical panes: `Proxy log` and `Llama-server log`.  
2. SSE endpoints: either a merged SSE stream containing a `source` field, or a separate SSE endpoint `/logs/llama/tail` that returns identical message shapes as `/logs/tail`.  
3. Both panes support: initial N lines, append streaming, autoscroll, clear, download (filename matches source), connect/disconnect.  
4. Server-side respects configured `log_dir` and gracefully handles missing/rotated files (send `{info: 'log_rotated_or_removed'}` or similar).  
5. Tests: smoke test that `/logs` returns 200 and that `/logs/tail?lines=1` returns initial payload when a log file exists (add a small unit/integration test or a simple assertion if present test harness exists).  

Appendix — Clarifying questions and answers
-------------------------------------------
- Q: "How should the llama-server log be sourced and exposed to the web UI?" — Answer (user): "Proxy server to combine both logs into a single stream". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.  
- Q: "Should the llama-server pane have the same controls as the proxy pane (lines, connect/disconnect, clear, download, autoscroll)?" — Answer (user): "Yes — same controls (recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.  
- Q: "Worklog (wl) DB migration: Worklog commands failed due to a DB schema mismatch. How should I proceed?" — Answer (user): "Proceed now without updating wl (draft intake file only) (recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.  

Potentially related docs
------------------------
- server.py - Implements `/logs`, `/logs/tail`, SSE handling and UI HTML.  
- README.md - Describes `proxy.log` and `llama-server.log` locations and tail commands.  
- proxyctl - CLI helper with `logs` command; references log directory.  
- config.yaml - Log directory and retention settings.

Potentially related work items
-----------------------------
- No matching work items found in local Worklog (wl search returned no results). (Worklog DB: pending migrations prevented creating/updating work items in this run.)

Notes about worklog (WL) operations
----------------------------------
- Attempts to create/update the work item via `wl` failed due to a local Worklog DB schema mismatch (pending migrations). The user instructed to proceed with draft-only intake. Once WL is fixed (run `wl doctor upgrade --confirm` or otherwise repair), apply this file as the work item description with `wl create` or `wl update --description-file` and set stage/status as appropriate.

data.description:
# Intake Brief: Proxy Blocks When Responding to a Request

## Problem Statement
The proxy blocks when the `/llama/local/status` endpoint is called after an OpenAI API request completes. The first status check succeeds; subsequent calls hang indefinitely. This suggests blocking in httpx client management when connecting to a busy llama-server.

## Users
- Developers and operators using the proxy to route OpenAI-compatible requests to local llama-server
- Users monitoring model status via the `/llama/local/status` endpoint or web UI

Example user story: "As an operator, I need to query the llama-server status at any time, even when the server is actively processing a request, so I can monitor system health without causing disruptions."

## Success Criteria
1. The `/llama/local/status` endpoint returns within 5 seconds even when llama-server is busy processing requests
2. Multiple concurrent status requests do not block each other
3. The proxy remains responsive during streaming and non-streaming local requests
4. No connection exhaustion or deadlocks occur under concurrent load
5. Existing tests pass and new tests verify non-blocking behavior

## Constraints
- The llama-server is a separate process (possibly single-threaded) that handles model inference
- The proxy uses FastAPI with asyncio for async operations
- Status queries must not interfere with ongoing requests
- The fix should not require architectural changes to FastAPI or the existing async model

## Risks & Assumptions
- **Assumption**: The blocking is caused by httpx client behavior (connection limits, no pooling), not a fundamental deadlock in the async code.
- **Assumption**: The llama-server can respond to status queries even when busy processing a request (though it may be slower).
- **Risk**: Scope creep - fixing httpx client configuration may reveal other blocking issues that could expand the fix scope. Mitigation: Record additional findings as new linked work items rather than expanding this bug fix.
- **Risk**: The fix may require changing how httpx clients are created/shared, which could impact other parts of the proxy. Mitigation: Test thoroughly and ensure backward compatibility.
- **Failure mode**: If the fix requires longer timeouts or more connections, it may mask underlying performance issues in llama-server.

## Existing State
- `query_llama_status()` creates a new `httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0)` per call with no connection pooling
- Streaming handlers (`proxy_to_local`) create `httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=None)` per request
- `_periodic_broadcast_loop` calls `query_llama_status()` every 1 second for SSE broadcasts
- No httpx connection limits configured; default limits may cause exhaustion under concurrent load

## Desired Change
1. Configure httpx.AsyncClient with appropriate connection limits and reuse clients via pooling
2. Ensure all httpx operations have timeouts to prevent indefinite blocking
3. Add concurrent status request handling that doesn't block the proxy
4. Verify fix with tests that simulate concurrent streaming requests and status queries

## Related Work
- `proxy/server.py` — Main proxy implementation. Key sections: `query_llama_status()` (line 336) creates httpx.AsyncClient for status checks; streaming handlers (line 1230-1298) proxy to llama-server; SSE endpoints `/events` (line 3087) and `/logs/tail` broadcast status updates; `_periodic_broadcast_loop` (line 439) calls status queries every 1 second.
- `tests/test_llama_local_status.py` — Existing tests for the status endpoint covering server running/not running and model switch states.
- `LP-0MNTCBP4900652XO` — "Implement non-blocking model load and immediate 503 response" (in-progress, high priority). Related non-blocking work that addresses similar concurrency issues in model loading. Not a duplicate - this bug is specifically about status endpoint blocking, not model loading blocking.

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions and Answers
- Q: "Is the llama-server single-threaded or can it handle concurrent HTTP requests?" — Answer (agent inference): Unknown from code. The bug description suggests the llama-server may block new connections while processing requests. Need to verify with testing. Source: code review of `query_llama_status()` and streaming handlers.
- Q: "Does the issue occur with both streaming and non-streaming requests?" — Answer (agent inference): The bug report mentions "OpenAI request" which typically uses streaming. The blocking may be more apparent with streaming requests as they hold connections longer. Source: bug description.
- Q: "What is the expected behavior when llama-server is busy?" — Answer (agent inference): The status endpoint should return quickly with available information rather than blocking. Source: code analysis of `_periodic_broadcast_loop` which calls `query_llama_status()` every 1 second.
data.title: Proxy blocks when responding to a request

data.description:
# Investigate /logs endpoint hang during active streaming request

## Problem Statement
Requests to /logs hang indefinitely when an LLM streaming request is in progress. This is a separate issue from the /llama/local/status blocking issue (LP-0MNWEZMUX009XL9B) which was about httpx connection pooling.

## Root Cause Found
In proxy/server.py line 3728, view_logs() calls router_list_models() when router_mode is enabled. router_list_models() (line 719) creates a new httpx.AsyncClient and makes a blocking request to /models on llama-server with a 10 second timeout. If llama-server is busy with a streaming request, this blocks until the streaming completes (or timeout).

Additionally, the HTML template string replacement at line 3737 performs multiple string replacements on a very large HTML document which could also contribute to slow response times.

## Success Criteria
1. Fix the issue so /logs responds within reasonable time during active streaming
2. Add tests to verify /logs works during active streaming

## Related Work
- LP-0MNWEZMUX009XL9B: Fixed /llama/local/status blocking with connection pooling
data.title: Investigate /logs endpoint hang during active streaming request

Problem: The proxy periodically queries llama-server endpoints (/model, /status) and many deployments return 404 for those paths, causing repeated 404s in llama-server logs. This work item will add resilient endpoint discovery and caching to avoid repeatedly hitting endpoints that 404, and add /models and /v1/models as fallbacks.

Acceptance criteria:
- Add a resilient lookup in  that tries multiple endpoints and caches the last successful one to avoid repeated 404s.
- The periodic status poll should prefer the cached endpoint and avoid hammering endpoints that previously returned 404.
- Add unit tests or ensure existing tests pass.
- Create a branch and commit the changes referencing this work item.

Headline
Set `gemma4` as the system-wide default model while keeping GPT-oss available as a selectable option; update runtime, config, aliases, tests, and docs so the change is visible and reversible.

Problem statement
Set Gemma 4 (`gemma4`) as the system-wide default model while preserving GPT-oss as a selectable option; update runtime, configuration, aliases, tests, and docs so the system, operators, and developers observe the new default behavior.

Users
- Platform operators and SREs: need a predictable default model that is maintained in configuration and logs.
- Developers and feature owners (Casey alias): expect the default model to respond to the previously used alias and behave as documented.

Success criteria
- The application loads `gemma4` as the default model from config and at runtime without errors (verify by startup logs and status endpoint showing `gemma4` as active when default is used).
- The default pointer in configuration is updated to `gemma4` and examples/docs clearly show GPT-oss remains available as a selectable option.
- The `Casey` alias resolves to `gemma4` at runtime and requests using the `Casey` alias are served by `gemma4` (verify with integration test and runtime logs).
- Automated tests (unit and integration) are added/updated to validate default model selection and alias resolution; tests pass in CI and locally.
- All related documentation (README, code comments, docs site) is updated to reflect the new default and preserved GPT-oss option.

Constraints

Constraints
 - Identifier to use: `gemma4` (confirmed).
 - Scope: global immediate replacement of the default pointer (change runtime, config, and docs everywhere unless blocked by other work items).
 - Rollback approach: manual rollback acceptable (no dedicated rollback flag required per current decision).

 - Important: Do not remove GPT-oss or its configuration entries; GPT-oss must remain available as a selectable option in config and at runtime.

Existing state
- Worklog item: Add Gemma 4 as default in place of GPT-oss (LP-0MNXARX6A0045GJO) exists and is currently in stage `idea` and status `in-progress`.
- Repo evidence: configuration files and server code reference `default_model` and show examples using `qwen3`, `gpt120`, or other ids. Notable files:
  - `proxy/config.yaml` contains a `default_model` key (current value: "gpt120").
  - `proxy/README.md` documents `default_model: "qwen3"` as an example.
  - `proxy/server.py` contains runtime logic that loads and preloads a configured `default_model`.

Desired change
- Update configuration defaults and runtime references so `gemma4` is the canonical default model id (update the default pointer only).
- Ensure alias mapping includes `Casey` -> `gemma4`.
- Update tests and add integration/unit tests asserting default selection and alias resolution.
- Update README and any other docs that recommend or show the previous default; show GPT-oss as an available option in examples.

- Ensure GPT-oss remains present in configuration examples and selectable at runtime (do not delete existing GPT entries; update only the default pointer).

Risks & assumptions
- Risk: Default model change may impact downstream behaviour (latency, cost, compatibility). Mitigation: smoke-test critical flows and monitor latency and error rates after rollout.
- Risk: Alias conflicts with existing alias rules. Mitigation: run alias resolution tests and verify `Casey` maps uniquely to `gemma4`.
- Assumption: `gemma4` is already available to the runtime environment and loadable by the llama-server; if not, a small onboarding task is required.
- Scope-creep mitigation: record any additional requested changes (e.g., feature flags, staged rollout, migration of model assets) as separate linked work items rather than expanding this item.

Related work
- `LP-0MNXARX6A0045GJO` Add Gemma 4 as default in place of GPT-oss — primary work item (this item).
- `proxy/config.yaml` — contains `default_model` setting; will be updated as part of this change.
- `proxy/server.py` — runtime logic that loads `default_model`; requires code edits to preload or alias mapping.
- `README.md` — docs references to `default_model` examples to update.

- Potentially related work items discovered in repo search:
  - `LP-0MNA7FYH4001ZUR4` Router-mode llama-server integration — mentions default model ids and router preload behaviour.
  - `LP-0MNVM4L0C0033FS2` Provide REST status endpoint for llama.cpp server — returns currently loaded model; relevant for visibility/monitoring of default.
  - `LP-0MN8LU54M00774GG` Routing & alias behaviour for embeddings — related alias resolution concerns.


Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers

- Q: "Which model identifier should be set as the default in code/config?"
  — Answer (user): "gemma4 (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "What scope should this change have?"
  — Answer (user): "Global immediate replacement (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "What level of testing and rollback support should be added?"
  — Answer (user): "Manual rollback acceptable". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

Research & evidence (agent):
- Found `proxy/config.yaml` with `default_model: "gpt120"`. (file: `proxy/config.yaml`)
- Found `proxy/README.md` referencing `default_model: "qwen3"`. (file: `proxy/README.md`)
- Found logic in `proxy/server.py` that reads `default_model` and attempts to preload/ensure the model is loaded at startup. (file: `proxy/server.py`)

Notes on idempotence
- This draft includes the three clarifying Q/A pairs received during intake; re-running the intake should update these entries rather than duplicating them.

data.description:
# Assign custom system prompts to model aliases — Intake draft (LP-0MNXBI97B00773WF)

## Problem statement
Proxy currently routes requests by model name and alias but has no first-class, auditable mechanism to assign a model-specific system prompt. Without this, teams cannot centrally control agent "personalities" or default system-level behavior per alias, leading to inconsistent behavior across clients and increased client-side complexity.

## Users
- Internal integrators and product teams who want consistent agent behaviour per alias (e.g., `embeddings`, `assistant`, `code`).
- Operators who need deployable, versioned defaults for system prompts and a safe override mechanism.
- Developers building lightweight client agents who rely on server-side prompt injection to keep clients simple.

Example user stories
- As a product integrator, I want to set a default system prompt for the `assistant` alias so all clients using that alias inherit the same temperament and guardrails.
- As an operator, I want repository-managed default prompts and a local override path for per-deployment tweaks without changing the repo.
- As a developer, I want the proxy to apply the prompt deterministically so client code does not have to mirror server-side defaults.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Configuration schema: Each model entry in `proxy/config.yaml` may include a `system_prompt` object with `file` (path) and `mode` (`override` or `prepend`). Example:

```yaml
models:
  gemma4:
    aliases: ["gemma4", "assistant"]
    llama_model: gemma4
    type: local
    system_prompt:
      mode: "prepend"   # or "override"
      file: "proxy/prompts/assistant.txt"
```

2. Prompt file resolution: For a request targeting an alias, the proxy loads the configured prompt content using the following precedence: 1) local override folder (project-local, e.g. `.sorraAgents/prompts/<alias>.txt`), 2) repo default file path (e.g., `proxy/prompts/<file>`), 3) no prompt if none present. Behavior must follow `get_model_config()` alias precedence rules (exact aliases then wildcard patterns). Verification: unit tests that assert override behavior and precedence.

3. Prompt application modes: When `mode` is `override` the request's system message(s) are replaced by the file content. When `mode` is `prepend` the file content is inserted as the first system message before any client-supplied system messages. Verification: unit tests and a small integration test that checks the messages forwarded to the backend API contain the expected system message(s).

4. Tests & validation: Add unit tests for prompt file parsing/resolution and message composition, and at least one integration test showing a request to an alias receives the injected prompt. All new and existing tests must pass locally/CI. Verification: CI run and local `pytest` return zero failures.

5. Documentation: Update `proxy/README.md` (logs/config section), `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md` (adding examples), and add a short runbook note describing prompt file locations, security considerations, and how to apply overrides. Verification: PR includes updated docs and example prompt files.

## Constraints
- Do not change backend model formats or public client-facing API; only the server should inject/compose system messages before forwarding requests to backends.
- Prompt files must be treated as plaintext UTF-8; implementations must limit loaded prompt size (e.g., 64KB) and fail safely (log + skip) if size exceeded.
- Prompt resolution must be efficient (cache file contents but refresh on change or provide explicit admin reload if appropriate).
- Respect existing alias precedence and wildcard semantics in `get_model_config()`.

## Existing state
- Alias resolution and wildcard alias semantics are implemented in `proxy/proxy/server.py` (`get_model_config()`). See lines around `get_model_config()` for alias precedence.
- `proxy/config.yaml` already lists many models and aliases; `models:` entries are the natural place for `system_prompt` fields.
- `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md` documents adding models and aliases — useful for documenting prompt file placement and examples.
- There are existing tests that use aliases (e.g., `proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py`) that should be extended or used as examples for integration tests.

## Desired change (summary)
- Add `system_prompt` config support per model/alias (file + mode: override|prepend).
- Provide repo-default prompt storage under `proxy/prompts/` and support project-local overrides under a deployment-local folder (project root folder matching existing conventions e.g. `.sorraAgents/prompts/`).
- Implement server runtime behavior to resolve and load prompt files (with safe size limits), apply according to `mode`, and forward composed messages to backends.
- Add unit/integration tests and documentation updates.

## Minimal implementation notes
- Config schema: add optional `system_prompt` mapping to model entries in `proxy/config.yaml` (documented in `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md`).
- Prompt resolution: implement helper `resolve_system_prompt(alias_or_model)` that searches local override folder then repo prompts; cache results with invalidation TTL or file-watch.
- Message composition: update request handling path (where system messages are built) to call `resolve_system_prompt()` and either replace or prepend accordingly before forwarding. Keep logging and a debug endpoint to inspect applied prompts for QA.
- Tests: unit tests for `resolve_system_prompt()` and composition; integration test that posts a chat request and inspects forwarded payload to the backend.

## Related work (automated report)
- proxy/config.yaml — canonical models & aliases; relevant to where `system_prompt` fields will live.
- proxy/MODEL_ADD.md — guide for adding models/aliases and examples; useful for docs and integration test guidance.
- proxy/proxy/server.py — `get_model_config()` implements alias precedence logic that must be preserved. (see `get_model_config()` near line ~1468)
- templates/gpt-oss-120b-opencode.mustache — contains system-message templating patterns which may be relevant to how prompts are formatted for some models.
- Worklog: LP-0MNTB69NS0031ZO2 — "Support friendly model aliases: embeddings, plan, code" (related feature for alias UX). 
- Worklog: LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC — "Add an embeddings specific model" (precedent for adding alias-backed models and tests).

## Risks & assumptions
- Risk: scope creep from adding more prompt modes or runtime UI; mitigation: create follow-up work items for additional features.
- Risk: Misapplication of prompts (e.g., accidentally overriding user-supplied system messages) — mitigation: conservative defaults (use `prepend` where unsure) and explicit `mode` setting; tests to cover both modes.
- Risk: Large prompt files increase memory or upstream token usage — mitigation: enforce size limit and document recommended prompt lengths.
- Risk: Wildcard alias ambiguity — mitigation: reuse `get_model_config()` precedence and include tests that demonstrate expected behavior.
- Assumptions: repository has an existing convention for local per-deployment folders (e.g., `.sorraAgents`); if not, the implementer will pick a sensible path and note it in docs.

## Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
- Q: "Local override path — Which do you prefer as the default override folder (I will make this configurable in code/config): A) .sorraAgents/prompts/ (recommended; matches intake) B) .opencode/prompts/ (if you prefer OpenCode defaults) C) Make override path configurable via env/config and default to .sorraAgents/prompts/ D) Other (please specify path)"
  - Answer (user): "A" — Use `.sorraAgents/prompts/`.

- Q: "system_prompt.mode default behavior — if a model entry omits `system_prompt.mode`, what should the server do? A) Default to 'prepend' (safe; preserves client system messages) B) Default to 'override' C) Treat missing mode as a validation error (require explicit mode)"
  - Answer (user): "C" — Treat missing mode as validation error (require explicit mode).

- Q: "Prompt caching / reload strategy — intake suggests caching but unknowns remain. Which approach should we implement initially? A) TTL-based cache (configurable, default e.g. 30s) + optional admin reload endpoint B) File-watch / inotify to invalidate immediately C) No automatic refresh — require admin reload or process restart (simplest)"
  - Answer (user): "No cache, reload on every use" — read files on each resolve call.

## Plan: changelog
- 2026-06-03T08:54:49Z — Created child: Prompt resolution tests (LP-0MPXTYWL70047KKD)
- 2026-06-03T08:54:54Z — Created child: Prompt composition tests (LP-0MPXTZ0BQ0010ESI)
- 2026-06-03T08:54:58Z — Created child: Prompt injection integration test (LP-0MPXTZ3HM00049F9)
- 2026-06-03T08:55:03Z — Created child: Config schema & validation (LP-0MPXTZ71W0024ISW)
- 2026-06-03T08:55:07Z — Created child: Prompt resolver (LP-0MPXTZAG0001BMHU)
- 2026-06-03T08:55:12Z — Created child: Message injection (LP-0MPXTZE450024RVI)
- 2026-06-03T08:55:16Z — Created child: Prompt debug endpoint (LP-0MPXTZHJM006S1XE)
- 2026-06-03T08:55:21Z — Created child: Docs & examples (LP-0MPXTZL9I001Q7CN)

## Dependencies added
- Implementation items were linked to their test items so test authoring is favored before implementation. Notable edges:
  - Config schema & validation (LP-0MPXTZ71W0024ISW) depends on Prompt resolution tests (LP-0MPXTYWL70047KKD)
  - Prompt resolver (LP-0MPXTZAG0001BMHU) depends on Prompt resolution tests (LP-0MPXTYWL70047KKD)
  - Message injection (LP-0MPXTZE450024RVI) depends on Prompt composition tests (LP-0MPXTZ0BQ0010ESI) and Prompt injection integration test (LP-0MPXTZ3HM00049F9)
  - Prompt debug endpoint (LP-0MPXTZHJM006S1XE) depends on Message injection (LP-0MPXTZE450024RVI) and Prompt composition tests (LP-0MPXTZ0BQ0010ESI)
  - Docs & examples (LP-0MPXTZL9I001Q7CN) depends on Message injection (LP-0MPXTZE450024RVI)

*(End of plan changelog)*

Summary:
All models are failing to load via the proxy when requested by clients. This blocks model usage across services.

Observed behavior:
- Model load attempts through the proxy return errors or time out.
- Failure appears to affect all model backends behind the proxy.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Send a model load request through the proxy (example endpoint and payload if available).
2. Observe proxy response or timeout.

Expected behavior:
- The proxy should route requests to model backends and return successful model load responses.

Investigation approach:
- Collect proxy logs and model backend logs around failure times.
- Search repository for proxy implementation and recent changes affecting routing, auth, or TLS.
- Run unit/integration tests for proxy and model loading code.
- Verify configuration (endpoints, credentials, certificates) used by the proxy.
- Attempt to reproduce locally if possible.

Acceptance criteria:
- Root cause identified and documented.
- A fix or mitigation is proposed/implemented and tested, or a clear next-step plan created.
- Logs, relevant code references, and the commit/PR that addresses the issue are added to this work item.
data.title: Investigate proxy model load failures

data.description:
Headline
--------
Add router-only unit tests that verify deterministic model load/unload transitions (embed + primary slot), ensure embedding requests do not trigger primary switches, and validate stable error/unload states.

Problem statement
-----------------
The repository lacks a focused, router-only unit test suite that proves deterministic model load and unload transitions for the embeddings slot + primary model. Without these tests, race conditions and non-deterministic behaviour during concurrent or error-prone model lifecycle events may regress co-residency and router-mode features.

Users
-----
- Engine developers and maintainers working on proxy/server.py lifecycle code.
- CI and QA engineers who rely on unit tests to catch regressions during refactors.
- Operators who need confidence that embedding requests will not unexpectedly switch the primary model.

Example user stories
- As an engineer, I can run unit tests that assert load state transitions for the embed alias are deterministic in router-mode.
- As an integration tester, I can run a lightweight stub-based test that simulates a llama-server stub to verify the proxy's state machine under a realistic load.
- As an operator, I expect embedding requests not to cause a primary model switch when co-residency is enabled; tests should assert this behaviour.

Success criteria
----------------
- New unit tests live at proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py and cover: deterministic state transitions for loading (requested → loading → loaded), that embedding requests do not trigger primary switches when co-residency is enabled, unload/reload transitions, and failed load handling leaving a stable error state.
- At least one lightweight stub-based integration-style test is present (in the same test file or proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_integration.py) that runs against a minimal HTTP stub of llama-server to validate the state machine end-to-end.
- Tests are deterministic and CI-friendly (mocked HTTP for unit tests; the stub test is allowed but marked as slow if necessary). All added tests pass locally and in CI on the default test runner.
- All related documentation (test file headers, proxy/README.md where tests are referenced, and any code comments) is updated to reflect the new tests.
- Full project test suite must pass with the new changes.

Constraints
-----------
- Scope: Router-mode only; do not modify non-router mode behavior in this task.
- Test design: Prefer pure unit tests with mocks for the state machine and HTTP responses; include a single stub-based integration-style test for realism (mix approach selected).
- Keep tests lightweight and deterministic; avoid long sleep-based waits where possible.

Existing state
--------------
- proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py contains router-mode unit tests and helper patterns that the new tests should follow and extend.
- proxy/server.py implements lifecycle helpers: ensure_model_loaded, schedule_background_load, router_load_model, router_wait_for_model, and global runtime state (current_model, background_loads). These are the implementation targets.
- Related parent/ancestor work exists: LP-0MNA7G07U0020NDC (Model lifecycle: embed + primary slot) and LP-0MN8LU516006M2SN (Co-residency runtime support). A co-residency spike (LP-0MN8SI4S5004AQVX) produced memory guidance and related notes.

Desired change
--------------
- Add a new test file: proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py containing unit tests that:
  - Use pytest + pytest-asyncio and monkeypatch to stub start/wait/router functions and to simulate HTTP responses for llama-server endpoints when required.
  - Implement tests for the scenarios selected: (1) deterministic load state transitions; (2) embedding requests do not trigger primary model switches; (4) unload/reload deterministic behaviour; (5) failed load leaves stable error state.
- Add a single stub-based integration-style test (in the same test file or a sibling file) that runs a minimal HTTP stub server to emulate llama-server lifecycle endpoints and verifies end-to-end behaviour for one or two scenarios.
- Update proxy/README.md (tests section) with instructions to run the new router-only tests and note any slow/stub tests.

Related work (automated report)
-------------------------------
- LP-0MNA7G07U0020NDC — Model lifecycle: embed + primary slot (parent). Describes the broader feature, acceptance criteria, and references existing tests. This child task implements the deterministic unit test coverage requested by the parent. (LP-0MNA7G07U0020NDC)
- LP-0MN8LU516006M2SN — Co-residency runtime support (ancestor). Contains design notes, constraints, and requested tests for router-mode lifecycle; relevant for test scenarios and risk considerations. (LP-0MN8LU516006M2SN)
- LP-0MN8SI4S5004AQVX — Co-residency spike: llama-server API & memory (completed). Provides runtime observations and artifacts for choosing a model pair and measuring memory; useful for deciding which behaviors to stub in integration-style tests. (LP-0MN8SI4S5004AQVX)
- LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC — Add an embeddings specific model (completed). Documents aliasing and examples used in existing tests and configs; useful for sample model names and expected resolution semantics. (LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC)
- proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py — Existing unit tests and patterns to extend. Demonstrates usage of monkeypatch and background loader markers; good examples to follow. (proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py)
- proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py — Integration test patterns for embeddings; useful for deciding stub behaviour and expectations for embeddings responses. (proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py)

Risks & assumptions
-------------------
- Risk: Tests may rely on internal global state in proxy/server.py which can lead to brittle tests if implementation changes. Mitigation: reset module-level state at test setup and use monkeypatch to replace external dependencies.
- Risk: Slow or flaky tests if integration stub uses sleep-based timing. Mitigation: prefer mocked unit tests and keep stub test minimal and timeboxed; mark slow tests to be excluded by default in fast CI runs.
- Risk: Scope creep — adding broader integration tests or modifying runtime logic. Mitigation: restrict this work item to router-only unit tests and one minimal stub test; any additional tests become separate follow-ups.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
--------------------------------------
- Q: "Where should the new unit tests live? (pick one)
- A: Extend proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py (add router-only cases)
- B: New file proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py (keeps router-only tests separate)
- C: Other — please give path." — Answer (user): "B" (user). Source: interactive reply. Final: New file `proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py`.

- Q: "Which minimal scenarios must be covered by the deterministic lifecycle tests? (pick one or more)
- 1: Load embed model (router-only) and assert state transitions: requested → loading → loaded
- 2: Ensure embedding request does NOT trigger primary model switch when co-residency enabled
- 3: Concurrent requests: simultaneous embed + primary requests produce deterministic ordering (no race/flip-flop)
- 4: Unload/reload transitions: ensure unload leads to expected state and subsequent loads are deterministic
- 5: Error handling: failed load yields stable error state and does not leave partial/mixed state" — Answer (user): "items 1, 2, 4 and 5" (user). Source: interactive reply. Final: Include scenarios 1, 2, 4, 5.

- Q: "Test style: should these be pure unit tests that mock llama-server / HTTP calls, or should we run against a lightweight stub process?
- A: Pure unit tests with pytest + pytest-asyncio, mocking HTTP (fast, CI-friendly) — recommended
- B: Lightweight stub llama-server (more realistic, slower)
- C: Mix: unit tests for state machines + one integration-style test with stub" — Answer (user): "C" (user). Source: interactive reply. Final: Mix approach; prefer unit tests + one stub-based integration-style test.

Appendix: End.
data.title: Add unit tests for deterministic model load/unload transitions (router-only)

## Acceptance Criteria

Summary: Unit tests ensuring embedding requests do not trigger a primary model switch when co-residency/pinning is enabled (embed vs gemma4).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests verify that POST /v1/embeddings (create_embeddings) does not change server.current_model when co-residency is enabled.
- Test covers both: (a) embeddings model already loaded (serve immediately), (b) embeddings model not loaded (schedule background load and return 503) without switching primary.
- Tests assert schedule_background_load is called in the expected case and that response payload contains model_loading details when 503.

Minimal implementation:
- Unit tests that monkeypatch router_is_model_loaded, schedule_background_load, proxy_to_local, and related helpers.
- Use existing test patterns and reset module-level state.

Dependencies: Deterministic load transitions test patterns (reuse).

Deliverables: tests, assertions, README test instructions.

Summary: Unit tests that simulate model load failures and assert a stable error state with no partial/mixed state left behind.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests simulate router_load_model or start_llama_server failing and assert either previous current_model is retained (if expected) or current_model is None per design, background_loads cleared, and last_start_failure or an error broadcast is set.
- Tests assert no lingering background_loads markers and model_switch_refcount is decremented appropriately.

Minimal implementation:
- Unit tests that monkeypatch router_load_model/start_llama_server to return False or raise, and monkeypatch broadcast_status to capture 'error'.
- Reset module-level state in setup/teardown.

Deliverables: tests and captured assertions.

data.title: [test-failure] test_router_mode_serves_embeddings_and_chat — failing test

data.description:
Problem statement:
Implement session-based incremental ingestion in our llama-server client/proxy integration so that the server reuses a per-session KV cache and ingests only new tokens, instead of re-ingesting the full conversation on every request. This reduces CPU and latency for multi-turn conversations.

Users:
- Proxy users and clients making multi-turn requests to llama-server (internal services, Python clients, curl-based tests).
- Example user story: "As a client developer, I want the proxy to reuse a session so that subsequent messages in a conversation are fast and use less CPU."

Success criteria:
- The proxy/client can create and reuse a `session_id` so llama-server persists KV cache across requests.
- Subsequent requests for the same session send only new messages/tokens; logs show llama-server ingesting only the new tokens.
- A Python client example and a curl example demonstrate session creation, reuse, and observable reduction in ingestion time.
- Performance validation: example benchmark shows measurable reduction (e.g., >= 50% CPU time or ingestion time) vs full-history re-ingestion for multi-turn scenarios.
- All related documentation is updated; full project test suite passes.

Constraints:
- Editing earlier messages invalidates the KV cache; callers must re-ingest full history when edits occur.
- Context window and KV cache eviction limits imposed by llama-server must be respected.
- Sessions are ephemeral (in-memory) unless persisted by llama-server; cross-restart durability is not required for initial implementation.

Existing state:
- Current client/proxy re-sends full conversation on each request; a draft work item exists describing session-based incremental ingestion (LP-0MO6DJP0P006WCHA).
- Repo contains llama-server docs (LLAMA_README.md), router docs (docs/llama-router.md), and proxy implementation files (proxy/proxy/server.py) that currently interact with llama-server.

Desired change:
- Update proxy/client to generate/manage `session_id` values and forward only incremental messages to llama-server when a session is reused.
- Provide example clients (Python, curl), a minimal proxy helper, and documentation.
- Add tests/benchmarks validating reduced ingestion.

Risks & assumptions:
- Risk: Editing earlier messages requires full re-ingestion and may invalidate per-session KV caches. Mitigation: Document this limitation and provide client guidance; record follow-up work if edit-preserve becomes required.
- Risk: Sessions held in memory may increase memory usage and require eviction policies. Mitigation: Define session lifetime and eviction/rotation policy; log metrics to observe memory usage.
- Risk: Misuse (clients accidentally resending full history) could negate benefits. Mitigation: Proxy should detect and strip duplicate history when safe, and document recommended client patterns.
- Assumption: llama-server supports per-session KV caches keyed by `session_id` (confirmed by existing work item description). Source: LP-0MO6DJP0P006WCHA description.
- Scope-scope creep mitigation: Any significant follow-ups (session persistence across restarts, cross-node session sharing, or edit-preserve features) will be recorded as separate work items linked to this parent.

Related work (automated report)
- LLAMA_README.md: Provides high-level guidance for running llama-server and how the proxy interacts with it. Relevant for integration and deployment notes.
- docs/llama-router.md: Documents router-mode operation for llama-server when hosting multiple models; relevant if sessions must be routed or sharded across router children.
- proxy/README.md: Describes how the proxy starts/manages llama-server and contains architecture context for how client requests are forwarded; useful for implementation details and examples.
- proxy/proxy/server.py: Primary proxy implementation that currently forwards requests to llama-server and manages SSE/status broadcasts. This is the main integration point for session handling and where `session_id` logic should be implemented.
- Work item LP-0MNWEZMUX009XL9B ("Proxy blocks when responding to a request"): Addresses connection pooling and non-blocking httpx client usage when talking to llama-server. Relevant because session-based incremental ingestion will interact with the same client plumbing and should reuse existing shared client and timeout/limits practices.
- Work item LP-0MNWV7I8M002IWL4 ("Fix repeated 404s querying llama-server status"): Describes resilient endpoint discovery and caching for llama-server status endpoints; useful when implementing query/fallback logic for session metadata or capabilities.
- Work item LP-0MN5AW8DE1KJVAKX ("Add stats to the main page for the currently loaded model"): Implements SSE broadcasting of model metadata (n_ctx, KV cache counts) and related tests; relevant for validation and telemetry during session reuse testing.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
- No clarifying questions were required; agent proceeded using the existing work item content as authoritative. (Answer source: work item LP-0MO6DJP0P006WCHA description.)
data.title: Implement incremental session-based prompt ingestion for llama-server

data.description:
## Summary
Implement hybrid session_id generation (client-supplied or proxy-generated) with 3-hour TTL and in-memory session registry.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Proxy accepts X-Session-Id header from client; if absent, proxy generates opaque UUID v4 session_id
- Proxy returns X-Session-Id header in response for all requests (echo client-provided or return generated)
- Sessions expire after 3 hours of inactivity; expired sessions are evicted from memory
- Session registry tracks: session_id, created_at, last_activity_at, message_count
- Unit tests cover generation, acceptance, TTL expiry, and eviction logic
- Negative case: When session_id is invalid/expired, proxy falls back to full history ingestion

## Minimal Implementation
- Add SessionManager class in proxy/proxy/server.py with dict-based in-memory storage
- Add session_id extraction from headers and generation fallback
- Add background cleanup task for expired sessions (simple periodic scan)
- Add response header injection for session_id
- Unit tests in proxy/tests/test_session_manager.py

## Dependencies
None (foundational feature)

## Deliverables
- Code: SessionManager class, header handling in request/response path
- Tests: test_session_manager.py (unit tests)
- Docs: Update proxy/README.md with session_id header documentation
data.title: Session ID generation and management core

data.description:
## Summary
Modify proxy to forward only new messages to llama-server when session_id is reused, leveraging llama-server's per-session KV cache.

## Acceptance Criteria
- When session_id is reused, proxy sends only new messages (not full conversation history) to llama-server
- llama-server logs show ingestion of only new tokens (verified via log parsing in tests)
- Proxy tracks per-session message history to determine new messages
- Full history is sent when session_id is new or when client indicates edit (no special edit handling in proxy)
- Integration test validates reduced payload size on subsequent requests
- Negative case: Benchmark fails gracefully if llama-server unavailable

## Minimal Implementation
- Extend SessionManager to store per-session message list (role, content tuples)
- Modify chat completion request handler to compare incoming messages vs stored history
- Compute delta (new messages only) and forward delta to llama-server
- Update session history after successful llama-server response
- Integration test in proxy/tests/test_incremental_ingestion.py

## Dependencies
LP-0MO6EWJ5K0088CBS (Session ID generation and management core)

## Deliverables
- Code: Delta computation logic, history tracking in SessionManager
- Tests: Integration test validating incremental vs full payload
- Docs: Add example to proxy/README.md showing incremental request pattern

data.title: Documentation updates and edit-behavior guidance

data.description:
Implement prevention strategies for proxy saturation:

1. Add proxy timeout to prevent indefinite hangs when llama-server is overloaded
2. Cap concurrent requests to limit load on llama-server slots
3. Reduce context sizes in models.ini to prevent massive prompt re-processing delays
4. Check llama-server slot availability before forwarding requests
5. Fix router model loading check to verify status is 'loaded' not just present in list

This addresses the issue where Qwen3 llama-server got stuck re-processing a 41k-token prompt, causing all 4 slots to be busy and new requests to time out.
data.title: Add proxy timeouts and concurrency limits

data.description: Bug: In proxy/server.py line 4047, the JavaScript regex literal inside the HTML raw string uses  instead of . This causes the browser to throw 'Uncaught SyntaxError: Invalid regular expression flags' because the unescaped  terminates the regex early. The JS crashes before SSE connections are established, so logs do not stream and the connect button appears unresponsive. Fix by changing  to  in the Python raw string so the rendered HTML contains valid JS regex .

data.description:
Summary:

A backend connection error (proxy error: Failed to read connection at 2026-05-26 23:10:11) coincided with multiple overlapping /v1/chat/completions requests and repeated 'Max concurrent queries reached (8/8)' rejections. We cannot access the Pi instances from this environment, so operator assistance is required to collect host-side logs and evidence.

Goal:

Determine the root cause of the backend read/connection failures and why requests were held open/retried, and produce remediation steps to avoid proxy-level 503 overloads.

What to collect / reproduce steps (operator):
1. Provide llama-server logs (/var/log/llama-proxy/llama-server.log) around 2026-05-26 23:09:30..23:11:00. Include rotated files if needed.
2. Provide any system logs (journalctl -u llama-server or systemd journal) covering the same window.
3. Confirm whether any compaction/summarization or other agent activity ran at the same time (cron, systemd timers, or Pi agents).
4. If possible, provide network-level logs or tcpdump of connections to localhost:8080 around that time.
5. Confirm llama-server resource stats (memory/CPU) at that time or nearest sample.

Acceptance criteria:
- Root cause identified (e.g., llama-server crash/hang, slot exhaustion, OOM, transient network error) with supporting log excerpts.
- If caused by llama-server instability, a recommended fix/action plan (restart policy, memory limits, slot tuning, or code fix).
- If caused by client retries/increased concurrency, recommendations for mitigating retries, client throttling, or increasing max_concurrent_queries with estimated capacity.

Notes:
- I (the agent) cannot access the Pi instances to gather the logs; operator must attach logs or run the commands above and paste outputs or grant access.
data.title: Investigate llama-server backend connection errors causing proxy retries and concurrency spike

data.description:
Summary:

Logs indicate session-based cache restore is not functioning during the incident: log message 'history match (2 new messages) — sending full prompt, session_id-based cache restore not functioning'. This causes larger payloads, higher latency per request, and contributes to concurrent query overlap.

Goal:

Investigate why session_id-based cache restore is failing and implement a fix so that: when a request includes a session id and the llama-server can restore the KV cache for that session, the proxy uses incremental ingestion correctly (or documents when it cannot).

What to collect / reproduce steps (operator):
1. Provide proxy logs showing the 'history match ... session_id-based cache restore not functioning' messages with surrounding request bodies.
2. Provide llama-server logs during the same requests to check for 'slot load_session' or LCP messages that indicate cache restore behavior.
3. If feasible, capture a minimal reproducible sequence: create a session via a first request, then send a subsequent request with the same X-Session-Id header and verify whether llama-server performs a KV cache restore. Attach request bodies.

Acceptance criteria:
- Root cause identified and documented (bug in proxy session_manager.compute_delta logic; header formatting; llama-server compatibility; or ordering/timing issue).
- Fix implemented (code change) so that when the session is not edited and message history matches, either: (a) delta-based ingestion is used correctly, or (b) the proxy logs a clear reason why delta cannot be used and ensures the full prompt handling is optimal.
- Unit/integration tests added that cover session restore success and failure cases, verifying session history maintenance and reduced payload size on repeated requests.
- Manual verification steps documented so operator can confirm in their environment (since I cannot access the Pi instances).

Notes:
- I cannot access the Pi instances to reproduce or fetch logs; operator must attach logs or run the minimal reproduction steps above and provide outputs.
data.title: Fix session-based cache restore not functioning (session manager)

data.description:
Mitigate backend crash cascades by hardening both proxy behavior and runtime policy so a llama-server GPU fault does not trigger prolonged retry storms and proxy saturation.

## Problem statement
A llama-server ROCm crash path can currently cascade into proxy read/connect failures and concurrency saturation, causing repeated request rejection and poor recovery behavior. The work must reduce blast radius by combining proxy-side safeguards with runtime/service policy controls.

## Users
- Primary: On-call engineers operating the local proxy+llama stack during incident response.
- Secondary: Developers using `/v1/chat/completions` locally who need predictable failure/retry behavior.

User stories:
- As an on-call engineer, I want backend crash conditions to fail fast with controlled retries so the proxy remains responsive.
- As a developer, I want clear signals when backend capacity is reduced so clients can retry safely.

## Acceptance Criteria
- When a backend connection/read failure pattern occurs, proxy retries are bounded with backoff+jitter and do not immediately fan out; behavior is verified by automated tests in `proxy/tests` and a reproducible fault-injection run with captured logs.
- Proxy concurrency guard is reduced and enforced to a configured value aligned with backend capacity (target baseline: `max_concurrent_queries` = 3–4), and rejection behavior remains explicit/consistent under load.
- Runtime/service policy adds watchdog + health-gated readiness so router/child llama-server process faults recover without long unavailable windows; readiness state is observable in logs/health endpoints.
- Observability includes explicit, queryable signals for backend read/connect failures and concurrency-gate rejects, with documentation of expected log signatures for triage.
- All related documentation is updated to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant wiki or docs site entries.
- Full project test suite must pass with the new changes.

## Constraints
- In scope includes both proxy code and service/runtime policy changes; runtime policy must not bypass container/distrobox constraints already enforced by the proxy.
- Validation evidence must include automated tests and reproducible fault-injection evidence (not manual-only verification).
- Keep the feature scoped to crash-path mitigation; additional refactors/features discovered during implementation should be recorded as linked work items rather than added to this item.

## Existing state
- Proxy already has concurrency gates, slot checks, and model-loading 503/Retry-After behavior (`proxy/proxy/server.py`, `proxy/config.yaml`).
- Prior incident investigation confirmed crash sequence: ROCm launch failure -> backend read/connect errors -> proxy retry/concurrency saturation (`LP-0MPN836LQ002YQCY`).
- Router mode is enabled and constrained by `llama_models_max` and launch flags (`docs/llama-router.md`, `start-llama.sh`).

## Desired change
Add crash-path protections that combine: (1) bounded retry behavior with jitter after backend faults, (2) tighter concurrency policy to reduce thundering-herd pressure, (3) watchdog and health-gated readiness policy for runtime processes, and (4) stronger incident-facing observability and documentation.

## Related work
- `proxy/proxy/server.py` — current concurrency checks, slot checks, retry-after behavior, and health/status endpoints.
- `proxy/config.yaml` — current `max_concurrent_queries: 8`, timeouts, and router settings that need policy review.
- `proxy/README.md` — model loading, retry, and logging behavior documentation.
- `docs/llama-router.md` — router mode model co-residency constraints and operational behavior.
- `start-llama.sh` — router invocation flags (`--parallel`, `--models-max`) that affect effective capacity.
- Investigate llama-server backend connection errors causing proxy retries and concurrency spike (LP-0MPN836LQ002YQCY) — incident evidence and root-cause findings.
- Add proxy timeouts and concurrency limits (LP-0MPHM16H9002O54V) — prior hardening baseline for overload controls.
- Initial retry on model load is too long (LP-0MNXAHL1M007RSR9) — prior retry tuning context.
- Implement non-blocking model load and immediate 503 response (LP-0MNTCBP4900652XO) — existing non-blocking load behavior to preserve.

## Related work (automated report)
- `proxy/proxy/server.py` contains the active-query gate and slot-availability checks that currently reject load with 503; this is the primary control surface for adding bounded retry behavior and improved overload handling.
- `proxy/config.yaml` currently sets `max_concurrent_queries: 8` while router settings indicate lower effective backend parallelism; this mismatch is directly relevant to post-crash saturation and supports tuning toward a lower gate.
- Investigate llama-server backend connection errors causing proxy retries and concurrency spike (LP-0MPN836LQ002YQCY) provides direct incident evidence linking ROCm runtime failure to backend read/connect faults and subsequent proxy overload, making it the foundational context for this mitigation item.
- Add proxy timeouts and concurrency limits (LP-0MPHM16H9002O54V) and Implement non-blocking model load and immediate 503 response (LP-0MNTCBP4900652XO) are precedent hardening changes; this item should extend those mechanisms specifically for crash-path resilience rather than redesigning unrelated request routing behavior.
- `proxy/README.md` and `docs/llama-router.md` describe operator-visible retry/router behavior; both are directly affected by this work and must be updated so incident responders can distinguish expected degraded-mode behavior from new faults.

## Risks & assumptions
- Risk: Runtime watchdog policy may differ across environments; mitigation is to document environment-specific policy and expected health signals.
- Risk: Lowering concurrency too far can reduce throughput; mitigation is to capture baseline/fault-path behavior in reproducible tests and tune from evidence.
- Risk: Scope creep into broader resiliency redesign; mitigation is to create linked follow-up work items for out-of-scope opportunities.
- Assumption: Current backend effective capacity is lower than proxy gate under crash/retry conditions; this assumption should be validated during fault-injection runs.

## Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
- Q: "Primary target scope?" — Answer (user): "B" = proxy code safeguards + service/runtime policy. Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "What should count as done validation evidence?" — Answer (user): "A + B" = automated tests + fault-injection/repro script with logs. Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Concurrency policy direction after crash events?" — Answer (user): "B" = reduce gate (e.g., 3–4) to align with backend parallelism. Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
